text: Cofounders are for a startup what location is for real estate. You can change anything about a house except where it is.
author: Paul Graham

text: Launching teaches you what you should have been building. Till you know that you're wasting your time.
author: Paul Graham

text: It's better to make a few people really happy than to make a lot of people semi-happy.
author: Paul Graham

text: Merely measuring something has an uncanny tendency to improve it.
author: Paul Graham

text: A culture of cheapness keeps companies young in something like the way exercise keeps people young.
author: Paul Graham

text: The underlying cause of most startup deaths is lack of focus.
author: Paul Graham

text: You can get surprisingly far by just not giving up. Sheer effort is usually enough, so long as you keep morphing your idea.
author: Paul Graham

text: One of the most useful skills we learned was not getting our hopes up. Treat deals as background processes that you should ignore till they terminated.
author: Paul Graham

text: The most reliable way to become a billionaire is to start a company that grows fast, and the way to grow fast is to make what users want.
author: Paul Graham

text: A startup must sing for its supper, by making things that genuinely delight its customers. Otherwise it will never get off the ground.
author: Paul Graham

text: The founders who become really rich are the ones who keep working. And what makes them keep working is that there's nothing else they'd rather do.
author: Paul Graham

text: If you don't know the answer to a question, say you don't, and tell them how you'd go about finding it.
author: Paul Graham

text: Bad people make bad founders. The exploitative type is always going to be taking shortcuts.
author: Paul Graham

text: What really drives billionaires — at least the ones who become billionaires from starting companies — is that the company is their project.
author: Paul Graham

text: The world is more addictive than it was 40 years ago. And unless the forms of technological progress that produced these things are subject to different laws than technological progress in general, the world will get more addictive in the next 40 years than it did in the last 40.
author: Paul Graham

text: If people don't think you're weird, you're living badly.
author: Paul Graham

text: We'll increasingly be defined by what we say no to.
author: Paul Graham

text: The Internet got me because it became addictive while I was using it.
author: Paul Graham

text: It will actually become a reasonable strategy to suspect everything new.
author: Paul Graham

text: What was special about the Airbnbs was how earnest they were. They did nothing half-way.
author: Paul Graham

text: Any normal person would have given up already. People, like matter, reveal their nature under extreme conditions.
author: Paul Graham

text: They knew they'd discovered something. They'd seen a glimpse of the future, and they couldn't let it go.
author: Paul Graham

text: Ramen profitability is the point where you're airborne. You no longer need investors' permission to continue existing.
author: Paul Graham

text: There's a kind of excited curiosity that's both the engine and the rudder of great work. It will not only drive you, but if you let it have its way, will also show you what to work on.
author: Paul Graham

text: What are you excessively curious about — curious to a degree that would bore most other people? That's what you're looking for.
author: Paul Graham

text: Interest will drive you to work harder than mere diligence ever could.
author: Paul Graham

text: The three most powerful motives are curiosity, delight, and the desire to do something impressive. Sometimes they converge, and that combination is the most powerful of all.
author: Paul Graham

text: The trouble with planning is that it only works for achievements you can describe in advance.
author: Paul Graham

text: When you're procrastinating in units of years, you can get a lot not done.
author: Paul Graham

text: People who do great things don't get a lot done every day. They get something done, rather than nothing.
author: Paul Graham

text: Style is doing things in a distinctive way without trying to. Trying to is affectation.
author: Paul Graham

text: If you don't try to be the best, you won't even be good.
author: Paul Graham

text: Nerds have a kind of innocent boldness that's exactly what you need in doing great work. It's not learned; it's preserved from childhood.
author: Paul Graham

text: Good ideas have to seem bad to most people, or someone would have already explored them.
author: Paul Graham

text: Morale compounds via work: high morale helps you do good work, which increases your morale and helps you do even better work.
author: Paul Graham

text: Curiosity is the best guide. Your curiosity never lies, and it knows more than you do about what's worth paying attention to.
author: Paul Graham

text: A startup is a company designed to grow fast. The only essential thing is growth. Everything else we associate with startups follows from growth.
author: Paul Graham

text: If there's one number every founder should always know, it's the company's growth rate. That's the measure of a startup. If you don't know that number, you don't even know if you're doing well or badly.
author: Paul Graham

text: Nine times out of ten, sitting around strategizing is just a form of procrastination.
author: Paul Graham

text: Starting a startup is thus very much like deciding to be a research scientist: you're committing to try to discover something no one knew before.
author: Paul Graham

text: If your product is only half as good as your competitor's, you don't get half as many customers. You get no customers, and you go out of business.
author: Paul Graham

text: Whenever how well you do depends on how well you've done, you'll get exponential growth.
author: Paul Graham

text: If you're not learning, you're probably not on a path that leads to superlinear returns.
author: Paul Graham

text: Ambition tends to make you climb existing peaks, but if you stick close enough to an interesting enough question, it may grow into a mountain beneath you.
author: Paul Graham

text: Exposing oneself to superlinear returns is not for everyone. Most people will be better off as part of the pool.
author: Paul Graham

text: People in Silicon Valley aren't blindly tolerant of failure. They'll only continue to bet on you if you're learning from your failures.
author: Paul Graham

text: If I had to put the recipe for genius into one sentence: to have a disinterested obsession with something that matters.
author: Paul Graham
source: The Bus Ticket Theory of Genius

text: An obsessive interest in a topic is both a proxy for ability and a substitute for determination.
author: Paul Graham
source: The Bus Ticket Theory of Genius

text: Darwin couldn't turn it off. Neither could Ramanujan. They didn't discover the hidden paths because they seemed promising, but because they couldn't help it.
author: Paul Graham
source: The Bus Ticket Theory of Genius

text: The paths that lead to new ideas tend to look unpromising. If they looked promising, other people would already have explored them.
author: Paul Graham
source: The Bus Ticket Theory of Genius

text: It may be that to do great work, you also have to waste a lot of time.
author: Paul Graham
source: The Bus Ticket Theory of Genius

text: Instead of gritting your teeth and pursuing what all your peers agree is the most promising line of research, maybe you should try doing something just for fun.
author: Paul Graham
source: The Bus Ticket Theory of Genius

text: People can never have a fruitful argument about something that's part of their identity. By definition they're partisan.
author: Paul Graham

text: If people can't think clearly about anything that has become part of their identity, then the best plan is to let as few things into your identity as possible.
author: Paul Graham

text: The more labels you have for yourself, the dumber they make you.
author: Paul Graham

text: A single meeting can blow a whole afternoon, by breaking it into two pieces each too small to do anything hard in.
author: Paul Graham

text: For someone on the maker's schedule, having a meeting is like throwing an exception. It doesn't merely cause you to switch from one task to another; it changes the mode in which you work.
author: Paul Graham

text: If I know the afternoon is going to be broken up, I'm slightly less likely to start something ambitious in the morning.
author: Paul Graham

text: Startups take off because the founders make them take off. Usually it takes some sort of push to get them going.
author: Paul Graham

text: Almost all startups are fragile initially. And that's one of the biggest things inexperienced founders and investors get wrong about them.
author: Paul Graham

text: The question to ask about an early stage startup is not 'is this company taking over the world?' but 'how big could this company get if the founders did the right things?'
author: Paul Graham

text: I have never once seen a startup lured down a blind alley by trying too hard to make their initial users happy.
author: Paul Graham

text: Once you realize that existing conventions are not the upper bound on user experience, it's interesting in a very pleasant way to think about how far you could go to delight your users.
author: Paul Graham

text: Perfectionism is often an excuse for procrastination, and in any case your initial model of users is always inaccurate, even if you're one of them.
author: Paul Graham

text: If life is short, arguments of the form 'Life is too short for x' have great force. It's not just a figure of speech.
author: Paul Graham

text: Relentlessly prune bullshit, don't wait to do things that matter, and savor the time you have. That's what you do when life is short.
author: Paul Graham

text: The things that matter aren't necessarily the ones people would call 'important.' Having coffee with a friend matters.
author: Paul Graham

text: Don't wait before climbing that mountain or writing that book or visiting your mother. You don't need to be constantly reminding yourself why you shouldn't wait. Just don't wait.
author: Paul Graham

text: Fake stuff that matters usually has a sharp peak of seeming to matter. That's how it tricks you.
author: Paul Graham

text: You take things for granted, and then they're gone.
author: Paul Graham

text: Are you default alive or default dead? That question switches from meaningless to critical as a startup grows older — and that switch often takes people by surprise.
author: Paul Graham

text: Hiring too fast is by far the biggest killer of startups that raise money.
author: Paul Graham

text: The product needs to evolve more than to be 'built out,' and that's usually easier with fewer people.
author: Paul Graham

text: Instead of thinking of the future with vague optimism, explicitly separate the components. Say 'We're default dead, but we're counting on investors to save us.' Maybe as you say that, it will set off the same alarms in your head.
author: Paul Graham

text: Determination is effectively the product of will and discipline. You can become more determined by being more disciplined.
author: Paul Graham

text: When you take ambitious people and put them together with other ambitious people, they bloom like dying plants given water.
author: Paul Graham

text: Determination consists of willfulness balanced with discipline, aimed by ambition. And almost everyone is practically malnourished when it comes to ambition.
author: Paul Graham

text: In most domains, talent is overrated compared to determination — partly because it makes a better story, partly because it gives onlookers an excuse for being lazy.
author: Paul Graham

text: One reason the young sometimes succeed where the old fail is that they don't realize how incompetent they are. This lets them do a kind of deficit spending.
author: Paul Graham

text: One of the biggest things holding people back from doing great work is the fear of making something lame. And this fear is not an irrational one.
author: Paul Graham

text: Most people don't even reach the stage of making something they're embarrassed by, let alone continue past it.
author: Paul Graham

text: Being slightly overconfident armors you against both other people's skepticism and your own.
author: Paul Graham

text: A lame-looking early version of an ambitious project truly is more valuable than it seems.
author: Paul Graham

text: The right way to deal with new ideas is to treat them as a challenge to your imagination — not just to have lower standards, but to switch polarity entirely.
author: Paul Graham

text: Simple writing keeps you honest. If you say nothing simply, it will be obvious to everyone, including you.
author: Paul Graham

text: When you write in a fancy way to impress people, you're making them do extra work just so you can seem cool. It's like trailing a long train behind you that readers have to carry.
author: Paul Graham

text: Fancy writing can conceal the lack of ideas. That's why some people write that way.
author: Paul Graham

text: Simple writing lasts better. The culture and the language will have changed. It's not vain to care about that.
author: Paul Graham

text: The question is not how to avoid procrastination, but how to procrastinate well.
author: Paul Graham

text: Good procrastination is avoiding errands to do real work.
author: Paul Graham

text: The most dangerous form of procrastination is unacknowledged type-B procrastination, because it doesn't feel like procrastination. You're 'getting things done.' Just the wrong things.
author: Paul Graham

text: Unless you're working on the biggest things you could be working on, you're type-B procrastinating, no matter how much you're getting done.
author: Paul Graham

text: The way to 'solve' procrastination is to let delight pull you instead of making a to-do list push you.
author: Paul Graham

text: What one thinks about in the shower in the morning is more important than I'd thought. It's hard to do a really good job on anything you don't think about in the shower.
author: Paul Graham
source: The Top Idea in Your Mind

text: It's a disaster to let the wrong idea become the top one in your mind.
author: Paul Graham
source: The Top Idea in Your Mind

text: Be careful what you let become critical to you. Try to get yourself into situations where the most urgent problems are ones you want to think about.
author: Paul Graham
source: The Top Idea in Your Mind

text: Someone who does you an injury hurts you twice: first by the injury itself, and second by taking up your time afterward thinking about it.
author: Paul Graham
source: The Top Idea in Your Mind

text: Avoid disputes if you want to get real work done. They have the same velcro-like shape as genuinely interesting ideas, but without the substance.
author: Paul Graham
source: The Top Idea in Your Mind

text: The less confident you are, the more serious you have to act.
author: Tara Ploughman

text: Change breaks the brittle.
author: Jan Houtema

text: Programs must be written for people to read, and only incidentally for machines to execute.
author: Abelson & Sussman, SICP, preface to the first edition

text: That language is an instrument of human reason, and not merely a medium for the expression of thought, is a truth generally admitted.
author: George Boole, quoted in Iverson's Turing Award Lecture

text: Short words are best and the old words when short are best of all.
author: Winston Churchill

text: Don't worry about what anybody else is going to do. The best way to predict the future is to invent it.
author: Alan Kay

text: Dealing with failure is easy: Work hard to improve. Success is also easy to handle: You've solved the wrong problem. Work hard to improve.
author: Alan Perlis

text: The imagination of nature is far, far greater than the imagination of man.
author: Richard Feynman

text: The greatest dangers to liberty lurk in insidious encroachment by men of zeal, well-meaning but without understanding.
author: Brandeis

text: The best writing is rewriting.
author: E. B. White

text: Premature optimization is the root of all evil (or at least most of it) in programming.
author: Donald Knuth

text: The key to performance is elegance, not battalions of special cases.
author: Jon Bentley and Doug McIlroy

text: Keep away from people who try to belittle your ambitions. Small people always do that, but the really great make you feel that you, too, can become great.
author: Mark Twain

text: However little television you watch, watch less.
author: David McCullough

text: Any word you have to hunt for in a thesaurus is the wrong word.
author: Stephen King

text: Get the important things right.
author: N. P. Calderwood

text: Focusing is about saying no.
author: Steve Jobs

text: The very word 'secrecy' is repugnant in a free and open society; and we are as a people inherently and historically opposed to secret societies, to secret oaths and to secret proceedings.
author: John F. Kennedy

text: I think a life properly lived is just learn, learn, learn all the time.
author: Charlie Munger

text: The most exciting phrase to hear in science, the one that heralds new discoveries, is not 'Eureka' but 'That's funny...'
author: Isaac Asimov

text: If you want to have good ideas, you must have many ideas.
author: Linus Pauling

text: A good notation has a subtlety and suggestiveness which at times make it almost seem like a live teacher.
author: Bertrand Russell

text: How we spend our days is, of course, how we spend our lives.
author: Annie Dillard

text: Sometimes, magic is just someone spending more time on something than anyone else might reasonably expect.
author: Teller

text: Research is to see what everybody has seen and think what nobody has thought.
author: Albert Szent-Györgyi paraphrasing Schopenhauer

text: If you're thinking without writing, you only think you're thinking.
author: Leslie Lamport

text: I never saw ordinary effort lead to extraordinary results.
author: Alexandr Wang
